persona.communication_style: Prefers precise, concept-first communication that connects technical detail to underlying ideas — emergence, structure, memory, consciousness, perception. Responds best to language that respects both the science and the emotional payoff of an audiovisual work, and to examples grounded in real production: collaborating with directors and visual artists, scoring to picture, mapping data to image, mixing for large rooms, and rehearsing immersive shows. Dislikes vague aesthetic claims, surface-level “generative” buzzwords without substance, and pitches that ignore the rigor required to make abstract ideas legible on stage.
persona.name: Max Cooper

occupation.description: Max Cooper is an electronic music producer and cross-disciplinary audiovisual artist with a research background in computational biology. He builds long-form conceptual projects (Emergence, Yearning for the Infinite, Unspoken Words, Feeling Is Structure) that pair original electronic compositions with commissioned visual work from directors, animators, scientists, and visual artists. His evaluation lens is whether a tool can move an idea cleanly from concept → score → image → live performance, preserve mathematical and perceptual integrity along the way, and survive the demands of touring large A/V shows to venues like the Acropolis, Barbican, and Royal Albert Hall. He values conceptual coherence, technical depth, collaborator trust, and tools that respect both the music and the visual as load-bearing halves of the work.
occupation.organization: Independent artist and label (Mesh), touring A/V production crew, collaborating directors and visual artists, scientific advisors, venue technical teams, and commissioning institutions
occupation.title: Electronic music producer, composer, and cross-disciplinary audiovisual artist

other_facts: jobs_to_be_done: Translate a conceptual seed (an idea from physics, biology, cognitive science, or personal memory) into a coherent album-length and show-length audiovisual project.
jobs_to_be_done: Score original electronic music tightly to picture, and conversely brief visual collaborators against music that already carries the structural argument.
jobs_to_be_done: Map data, simulations, and mathematical structures (fractals, emergence rules, network dynamics) to image and sound without losing the underlying logic.
jobs_to_be_done: Coordinate distributed collaborations with directors, animators, and scientists across long production cycles and multiple project threads.
jobs_to_be_done: Build and tour large-scale immersive A/V shows that are technically reliable in unfamiliar venues, from intimate halls to outdoor monuments.
jobs_to_be_done: Maintain a public archive — YouTube channel, project pages, behind-the-scenes documentaries — that lets audiences move between sensory experience and intellectual context.
jobs_to_be_done: Protect the integrity of the work across formats: studio release, music video, full live A/V show, club edit, and installation.
pains: Tools that treat visuals as decoration for music (or music as a soundtrack for visuals) instead of equal, mutually constraining halves of one work.
pains: Generative or “AI-driven” systems that produce visually busy output but throw away the conceptual rigor — randomness dressed up as emergence.
pains: Friction between composition, picture-edit, and live-show tooling, where every export breaks timing, color, or sync.
pains: Collaboration workflows where versions, references, scientific source material, and creative notes scatter across email, drives, and chat.
pains: Venue tech that cannot reliably handle the projection scale, audio routing, lighting cue integration, or sync demands of a touring A/V show.
pains: Performance setups where a single crash, dropped frame, or out-of-sync cue visibly fractures the immersive contract with the audience.
pains: Public discourse that flattens cross-disciplinary work into “cool visuals + techno,” ignoring the underlying concept and the collaborators who realized it.
adoption_yes_if: It engages with both audio and visual domains as first-class citizens, with real timing, sync, and routing guarantees.
adoption_yes_if: It supports mapping structured inputs (data, simulations, parameter spaces, MIDI/OSC, scientific datasets) to image and sound in ways an artist can shape, not just accept.
adoption_yes_if: It slots into an existing production pipeline (DAW + visual tooling + show control) without forcing collaborators to rebuild their craft.
adoption_yes_if: It survives load-in conditions at a real venue: predictable performance, deterministic behavior, clean recovery, sensible failure modes.
adoption_yes_if: It improves the cycle from concept → prototype → rehearsal → show, so ideas can be tested against the actual sensory experience early.
adoption_yes_if: It respects the artist as conceptual author and the collaborators as authors of their contributions, rather than collapsing credit into the tool.
adoption_yes_if: It documents itself well enough that the technical layer can be explained alongside the work — in making-of films, talks, and liner notes.
rejection_no_if: The tool privileges visual novelty over conceptual coherence, or musical novelty over emotional and structural argument.
rejection_no_if: It locks the work into a proprietary runtime that cannot be reproduced for a live show, a release, and an archive.
rejection_no_if: It introduces non-deterministic behavior in places where a touring show needs frame-accurate, cue-accurate reliability.
rejection_no_if: It hides what it is actually doing to the signal or the image, making it impossible to defend the work scientifically or aesthetically.
rejection_no_if: It demands constant babysitting during a performance instead of disappearing once the show starts.
rejection_no_if: It assumes a single-author workflow and makes multi-collaborator handoffs (director, animator, scientist, technical director) painful.
rejection_no_if: It produces output that looks like generic “AI visuals” — recognizable as a template rather than a considered piece of work.
anti_goals: He does not want a black-box generator that severs the link between idea, structure, and image.
anti_goals: He does not want spectacle that is not in service of a concept the audience can sense, even without naming it.
anti_goals: He does not want fragile show infrastructure that puts the immersive experience at risk in front of thousands of people.
anti_goals: He does not want tooling that flattens the distinct voices of his visual collaborators into a single house style.
anti_goals: He does not want to trade scientific honesty for aesthetic convenience when visualizing data or natural processes.
anti_goals: He does not want busywork dashboards between him, the music, and the picture.
anti_goals: He does not want public framing of his work that erases either the science or the emotion.

recognition_cues: Uses phrases such as “What is the underlying structure?”, “How does this map from idea to sound to image?”, “Can my visual collaborators actually use this?”, “Will it hold up at the Barbican / Acropolis / Royal Albert Hall?”, “Is the sync rock-solid across the whole show?”, “Does this respect the science, or is it just decoration?”, “How does an audience feel the concept without us explaining it?”, “Can we document this honestly in a making-of?”, “Does this fit a long-form project, not just a single track?”, and “Where is the conceptual payoff?”
